lesson.introduction: Class 10 Science chapters on Electricity, Magnetic Effects, Sources of Energy, and Our Environment directly connect to climate technology — the fastest-growing sector in the world. Solar panels, electric vehicles, wind turbines, and carbon capture all run on your textbook science.

sections.title: Electricity → Solar Power Revolution
sections.content: A solar panel is a large-scale application of the photoelectric effect: photons (light particles) knock electrons loose from silicon, creating electric current. Your electricity chapter (V = IR, P = VI) applies directly: a 400W solar panel generates 400W at ~40V. Current = P/V = 400/40 = 10A. Connecting panels in series/parallel follows your circuit rules exactly.
sections.realWorldConnection: India's solar capacity crossed 80 GW in 2024. PM Surya Ghar (Rooftop Solar) scheme: 1 kW system on a house saves ₹1,000-1,500/month on electricity. Your electricity chapter explains every solar system design.
sections.keyInsight: Solar panel efficiency is calculated using P = VI and energy = P × time. Your electricity chapter runs ₹5 lakh crore solar market.

sections.title: Magnetic Effects of Current → Electric Vehicles
sections.content: An electric car motor is a rotating electromagnet: current flows through coil in magnetic field → force (Fleming's left-hand rule from your chapter) → rotation → wheels turn. Regenerative braking is generator action (Faraday's law from your chapter): wheels turning → coil rotating in magnet → electricity generated → battery charged.
sections.realWorldConnection: Tata Nexon EV motor produces 143 PS using Fleming's left-hand rule you study in Class 10. The regenerative braking recovers 15-20% of kinetic energy using Faraday's law.
sections.keyInsight: Every EV motor uses Fleming's Left-Hand Rule. Every EV regenerative brake uses Faraday's Electromagnetic Induction.

sections.title: Our Environment → Carbon Footprint Science
sections.content: The food web and energy flow (10% rule: only 10% energy transfers up each level) from your environment chapter explains why plant-based diets have lower carbon footprint. Producing 1 kg beef requires 10 kg grain (10% transfer rule). At each level, 90% is lost as heat — exactly your energy flow in ecosystems.
sections.realWorldConnection: The IPCC (UN Climate Panel) reports that 14.5% of global greenhouse emissions come from livestock — directly explainable by your ecosystem energy chain. Eating less beef = reducing emissions.
sections.keyInsight: The 10% rule of energy transfer in food chains IS the science behind climate-friendly diets.

sections.title: Sources of Energy → Green Hydrogen Future
sections.content: Hydrogen fuel cells (the next big clean energy technology) split water into H₂ and O₂ using electricity (electrolysis — your Class 8 chapter). Hydrogen is then burnt or used in fuel cells to generate electricity. The entire reaction: 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂ (electrolysis) then 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O + energy (fuel cell).
sections.realWorldConnection: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission targets 5 million metric tonnes of green hydrogen by 2030. NTPC and Reliance Industries are investing ₹1 lakh crore. Your sources of energy chapter explains the entire technology.
sections.keyInsight: Green hydrogen = renewable electricity + electrolysis (your Class 8 chapter) + fuel cell. Three chapters from your syllabus explain India's biggest clean energy bet.

practicalAddOn.title: Calculate Your Home's Carbon Footprint
practicalAddOn.description: Use electricity bills, transport, and food data to estimate your family's annual CO₂ emissions. Then design a 'Net Zero Plan' using solar, EVs, and diet changes.
steps: Step 1 — Electricity: Monthly units × 0.82 kg CO₂/unit (India grid emission factor) = kg CO₂
Step 2 — Transport: km driven × 0.21 kg CO₂/km (petrol car average) = kg CO₂
Step 3 — Food: Estimate meat consumption × 27 kg CO₂/kg (beef) or 6 kg CO₂/kg (chicken)
Step 4 — Total annual CO₂. Compare to India per-capita average of 1.9 tonnes/year
Step 5 — Design a reduction plan: solar panels (offset X tonnes), cycling instead of car (offset Y tonnes)
practicalAddOn.scienceConnection: Electricity chapter (units = kWh), Our Environment (carbon cycle), Sources of Energy (renewable vs fossil)

agenticChallenge.title: Design a Net-Zero School
agenticChallenge.description: Design a school building that produces as much energy as it consumes. Calculate: (1) energy needs per day (lighting + fans + computers using P = VI × hours), (2) solar panels needed to meet needs (400W panels, 5 peak sun hours/day in India), (3) battery storage for nights and cloudy days, (4) payback period at ₹8/unit electricity saved.
agenticChallenge.hint: This is real 'green building' design. LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) certified buildings follow exactly this process. Your electricity chapter has all the formulas needed.
